title: How to Work with Body Shame That Surfaces During Psychedelic Journeys
body:
How to Work with Body Shame That Surfaces During Psychedelic Journeys

So you’re on a psychedelic journey, right? The world is shifting, colors are bending, and then suddenly, out of nowhere, that old feeling creeps in: body shame. You feel exposed, maybe even trapped by those voices in your head telling you you’re not enough, your body is wrong, or you don’t deserve love. It’s raw, it’s real, and honestly, it can throw a wrench in the whole experience.

Look, this is a thing that happens to a lot of people. Psychedelic journeys have a way of peeling back layers you didn’t even realize were there. Body shame is one of those layers that lurks beneath the surface for many. It’s not just about how your body looks. It’s about stories you’ve carried, moments you’ve stored in your cells, moments society drilled into you. And when it shows up, it can feel overwhelming.

Here’s the thing: you don’t have to push through it or pretend it’s not there. You can work with it, gently, respectfully, and with curiosity. I’ll share practical ways to do that. Real, concrete tools that you can use during your journey or after it’s over. Sound good? Let’s get into it.

Recognizing Body Shame When It Surfaces

The first step is to recognize what’s happening. Maybe you’re lying down, your eyes closed, and suddenly your mind zooms in on a part of your body - your stomach, your thighs, your face - and the voice starts up: “You’re too fat,” “You’re ugly,” “You don’t deserve to feel good.” Right?! That voice can be loud, relentless.

Sometimes it’s not a voice but a feeling. A sinking weight in your chest or an unbearable itch of discomfort. You might notice yourself shrinking inside, curling up, wanting to disappear. Recognizing this feeling as body shame is key. It’s the door to working with it.

Ask yourself, “What am I actually feeling right now? Is this shame? Is it fear? Is it sadness?” Naming this experience can help you step back from it. It’s not easy, but that first step - naming - is powerful.

Why Body Shame Surfaces on Psychedelics

Truth is, psychedelics don’t create shame. They just show you what’s already there, often in sharper focus. Psychedelics lower your mental defenses, and that’s why these old wounds can bubble up. It’s like your psyche is saying, “Hey, you’ve been avoiding this for years. Let’s deal with it now.”

Sometimes it’s tied to trauma. Maybe you’ve been bullied for your body, or struggled with self-image for decades. Maybe it’s cultural messages, family stories, or a particular moment you remember vividly - like a high school dance when someone called you “fat” in front of everyone. Does that land?

Understanding why the shame is here helps you treat it with compassion rather than anger. You’re not broken. You’re human.

What to Do When Body Shame Hits During a Journey

When the shame hits, your immediate reaction might be to fight it, distract yourself, or shut down. All normal, but often those tactics just make the shame louder. Instead, try these approaches:

  • Breathe into the feeling. Sounds simple, but breathing deeply and slowly can help ground you. Try a 4-7-8 breath: breathe in for 4 seconds, hold for 7, exhale for 8. Repeat. Focus on the breath as a way to create a safe space inside your body.
  • Label the sensation. Say to yourself, “This is shame.” Putting a word on it can help you step back and watch it rather than get caught in it.
  • Talk to your body. If you can, gently place your hand on the part of your body that feels shameful. Say something soothing, like “I see you. I’m here with you.” It’s not about fixing anything, just presence.
  • Use grounding objects. A textured blanket, a smooth stone, or a soft pillow can help bring you back to the present moment. If you’re preparing for a journey, consider keeping these nearby.

There’s a simple exercise I sometimes guide people through: imagine the shame as a small child or animal. What does it look like? What does it want? Often, the shame is just hurt, wanting to be seen and held. This kind of compassionate curiosity can transform the experience.

Journaling After Your Journey

Once your journey winds down, writing can be a powerful tool. Don’t just jot down what you saw or felt - focus on the body shame that showed up. What triggered it? What did it want from you? What stories came up?

If you’re not sure how to start, a therapy journal with guided prompts (paid link) can guide you through this process gently, with questions that help you unpack complex feelings without getting overwhelmed.

Try writing without judgment. Let your hand move freely. Sometimes the act of putting shame on paper takes its power away.

Building a Supportive Environment

Look, working with body shame isn’t just about what happens when you’re tripping. It’s about the environment you create before and after. Here’s a few ideas that help me personally:

  • Set your space with care. I like using an aromatherapy essential oil diffuser (paid link) with lavender or frankincense. The aromas signal safety to my brain.
  • Screen your light exposure. Blue light can be overstimulating, especially post-journey. I use blue light blocking glasses (paid link) to help calm my nervous system in the evenings.
  • Have a trusted guide or sitter. Someone who knows you and can remind you that your body is okay. This person doesn’t have to be a psychedelic expert - just someone who can hold space for you without judgment.
  • Physical movement. Post-journey, gentle yoga, stretching, or even a slow walk can reconnect you with your body in a new way. The goal isn’t to “fix” but to be present.

When to Seek Professional Help

If body shame feels too heavy or you find yourself stuck in cycles of self-hate, professional support can be a game changer. Trauma often hides beneath shame, and healing trauma sometimes requires more than self-help tools.

Therapists who specialize in body image, trauma, or psychedelic integration can guide you safely through this process. Some therapists even incorporate psychedelic-assisted therapy in clinical settings - always with trained professionals and legal safeguards.

Here’s a resource that offers solid evidence-based info on trauma and shame from Johns Hopkins Medicine: Shame and Its Connection to Trauma. Worth a read if you want to understand the science behind what you’re feeling.

Integrating What You Learn

After your journey, integration is where the real work happens. It’s tempting to want to forget the hard feelings and just focus on the “good vibes.” But body shame deserves your attention. It’s a door to deeper healing.

Check in regularly with your body. Notice when shame tries to sneak back in. When it does, try to meet it with the same curiosity and kindness you practiced during your journey. It takes time, no doubt. But each moment of presence chips away at shame’s power.

If you want ideas on integration practices, check out my article How to Integrate Psychedelic Experiences. It’s full of practical tips and exercises you can start immediately.

Remember, This Is a Process

Body shame won’t vanish overnight. It’s a habit your mind learned. You’re rewiring it. Sometimes the shame will be loud. Sometimes quieter. Sometimes it feels like you’ve taken two steps forward and one step back. That’s normal.

Be patient. Be kind. And keep showing up for yourself.

If you want to learn more about navigating psychedelic journeys with honesty and care, visit my site anytime at kalesh.love. I’m here to walk alongside you.

Further Reading

If you want a well-written, deeply researched book that changed how many people see psychedelics, check out How to Change Your Mind by Michael Pollan (paid link). It’s a must-read for anyone curious about the potential and challenges of psychedelics.

Here’s the truth: working with body shame on psychedelics isn’t about fixing or erasing it. It’s about holding it gently, listening, and slowly rewriting the story you’ve been telling yourself for years. You’re worth that effort.

Disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider before making changes to your health regimen.
